narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, an LSAT score of 24, Year 1 school decile of 5, Year 3 decile of 4, and middle family income bracket, along with planning to attend full-time. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students, predicted an 83% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar. This high failure probability means the model saw your profile as carrying substantial risk compared to students who typically pass. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, which is well below the average of 36.7 seen in the dataset, signaling to the model a weaker foundation in the skills needed for bar success. Next in importance, your Year 1 decile of 5—lower than the typical 5.71—suggested early academic struggles that often correlate with bar challenges for students like you. Your Year 3 decile of 4, also below the average of 5.53, reinforced this by showing limited improvement over time, further tilting the prediction toward failure. While your middle income and full-time plans are common and didn't sway things much, these top three academic signals dominated the decision, outweighing other aspects of your background. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have higher scores and deciles indicating stronger performance, your metrics placed you in a higher-risk group. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience as a Hispanic man pursuing law—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, as it's the biggest lever here, and seek ways to strengthen your academic record, perhaps through post-bac courses or community college excellence. You might also explore bridge programs or schools with strong bar prep support tailored to diverse students. With focused improvements, you can shift future predictions in your favor and build the path to passing the bar. Keep pushing—your determination can rewrite this story.
